coreLaws.name: Fitts's Law
coreLaws.rule: Target size + distance affect interaction time
coreLaws.application: min-h-11 (44px) for touch targets on mobile

coreLaws.name: Hick's Law
coreLaws.rule: Decision time increases with number of options

coreLaws.name: Doherty Threshold
violations: No loading state for slow operations

coreLaws.name: Von Restorff Effect
coreLaws.application: Primary action must be visually distinct

coreLaws.name: Jakob's Law
coreLaws.rule: Users expect your site to work like others

coreLaws.name: Goal-Gradient Effect
coreLaws.rule: Users accelerate as they approach a goal
coreLaws.application: Show progress clearly, celebrate milestones, make completion feel achievable

coreLaws.name: Peak-End Rule
coreLaws.rule: Experiences are judged by peaks and endings
coreLaws.application: Design memorable moments and strong endings, invest in error recovery

coreLaws.name: Serial Position Effect
coreLaws.rule: First and last items are remembered best
coreLaws.application: Place most important items at start or end of lists

coreLaws.name: Zeigarnik Effect
coreLaws.rule: Incomplete tasks are remembered better than completed ones
coreLaws.application: Show incomplete progress, use draft indicators, leverage tension

coreLaws.name: Aesthetic-Usability Effect
coreLaws.rule: Aesthetically pleasing designs are perceived as more usable
coreLaws.application: Invest in visual polish, consistent styling builds trust

coreLaws.name: Postel's Law
coreLaws.rule: Be liberal in what you accept, conservative in what you send
coreLaws.application: Accept varied input formats, provide clear normalized output

coreLaws.name: Tesler's Law
coreLaws.rule: Complexity cannot be eliminated, only moved
coreLaws.application: Move complexity to the system, not the user

coreLaws.name: Cognitive Load
coreLaws.rule: Mental effort is finite - reduce unnecessary cognitive burden
coreLaws.application: Minimize choices, use recognition over recall, maintain context

coreLaws.name: Occam's Razor
coreLaws.rule: The simplest solution is usually the best
coreLaws.application: Prefer fewer features done well over many features done poorly

scanningBehavior.principle: Nobody reads. Everyone scans. Design for the scans, then write for the read.
scanningBehavior.emotion: Annoyance is a faster emotion than curiosity. If users can't find the point fast enough, they leave.

F-Pattern.behavior: Eyes scan top line fully, then progressively shorter lines down the left edge
F-Pattern.design: Front-load important content. First two words of headings matter most. Left-align key information.

Z-Pattern.when: Minimal content, hero sections, landing pages, marketing
Z-Pattern.behavior: Eyes go top-left → top-right → diagonal to bottom-left → bottom-right
Z-Pattern.design: Place logo/brand top-left, CTA top-right, supporting content bottom-left, primary action bottom-right.

contentJourney.description: Users go through three stages. Most never get past stage 1.

stages.name: Read
stages.goal: Reward them with clarity and a point

stages.name: Save
stages.goal: Give something structured enough to keep

visualHierarchy.principle: The mind seeks contrast. Without contrast, there is no hierarchy. Without hierarchy, everything competes for attention.

contrastTypes.rule: Big + Small — size difference creates importance ranking
Light + Bold — weight difference creates emphasis without changing size
contrastTypes.example: font-normal vs font-semibold at same size

contrastTypes.rule: Short + Shorter — progressive reduction signals hierarchy levels

negativeSpace.name: Rule 1: Negative Space
negativeSpace.principle: More space = faster scanning. Less space = more cognitive work.
negativeSpace.application: Whitespace is not wasted space — it is a functional element that accelerates comprehension
violations: Dense layouts with no breathing room

fontHierarchy.name: Rule 2: Font Hierarchy
fontHierarchy.principle: Use weight for emphasis, not size. Less words, more space. One point per view.
fontHierarchy.application: Limit to 2-3 font weights per view. Remove words until meaning breaks. Each screen has one takeaway.

gestaltPrinciples.name: Proximity
gestaltPrinciples.meaning: Close elements are perceived as related

gestaltPrinciples.name: Similarity
gestaltPrinciples.meaning: Similar elements are perceived as related

requirements: Primary button must be visually distinct
Green for feedback ONLY, not action
Most important at start or end

buttonColorSemantics.critical: Primary for action, Green for confirmation
rationale: Green fatigue: If every button is green, users develop blindness
Reserve green for feedback: Success toast has more impact when button wasn't green
Hierarchy > Semantics: Filled vs outline signals primary vs secondary
Industry convention: Apple, Google, Figma use brand color for main CTA

actionOverflow.critical: ≤3 actions = Visible buttons | ≥4 actions = Overflow menu

permissionBasedVisibility.rule: Disable don't hide. No gaps. All or nothing.

patterns.allUsers: Show all actions, disable if no permission
patterns.roleSpecific: Show only actions user's role can see (no gaps)

ATOM.description: Single UI elements - micro-interactions and visual distinction

MOLECULE.description: Grouped atoms - input handling, response time, progress feedback
MOLECULE.focus: Chunking, option limits, input flexibility, loading states, progress

ORGANISM.description: Complex sections - task completion and complexity management

TEMPLATE.description: Page layouts - conventions and user expectations

PAGE.description: Full experiences - journey design and simplicity
